reasoning: Q3 is 4.95x slower than DuckDB (380ms vs 77ms) with significant optimization headroom. Iteration 1 improved performance without regressions. With 1 iteration remaining, aggressively target the top 3 bottlenecks: (1) Bloom filter to eliminate 70-80% of unnecessary lineitem probes, (2) Swiss Tables to speed up 114K-group aggregation by 2-5x, (3) Compact hash tables to reduce join state from 600MB to 60MB for better cache locality. Expected combined speedup: 380ms → 220-270ms, closing gap to 2-3x.
focus_areas: Add Bloom filter on filtered orders to prune ~25M unnecessary bitmap probes during lineitem scan
Replace std::unordered_map with absl::flat_hash_map (Swiss Tables) for 2-5x faster aggregation
Replace sparse bitmap arrays with compact hash tables for join state (540MB → 50MB, better cache locality)
strategy_notes: This is an aggressive multi-pronged attack on the three bottlenecks identified by the Learner. All three optimizations are low-medium risk with proven techniques. Priority 1-2 are algorithmic improvements (Bloom filter + Swiss Tables) that don't change I/O patterns, so they're safe. Priority 3 (compact hash tables) has medium risk due to larger code changes but addresses a critical memory inefficiency. With only 1 iteration left after this, we need high-impact changes now. Skip I/O prefetching (Priority 4) since it requires more complex async I/O infrastructure and has uncertain gains on HDD.
notes: Assign these to specialized agents: Priority 1 & 3 → Join Optimizer (both are join-related optimizations). Priority 2 → Execution Optimizer (aggregation hash table replacement). Consider implementing Bloom filter first since it has the highest expected impact (60-80ms) and lowest risk. Swiss Tables is a simple drop-in replacement if abseil is available, otherwise implement Robin Hood hashing inline. Compact hash tables may require more extensive refactoring but the memory savings (600MB → 60MB) are critical for cache performance on 64-core system with 44MB L3.
